reviewer: new-demo build wave (in-browser runnability proof first, then headless-Chrome real download + real inference on the index page via CDP driver, mobile+desktop overflow + console/network checks, before/after visual review of the enhanced output)

rubric.evidence: REAL in-browser inference proven BEFORE building: an ORT-web@1.21.0 WASM harness (headless Chrome, no GPU) created a session from iat_lol_v1.onnx WITH its external-data sidecar (externalData:[{path:'iat_lol_v1.onnx.data', data}]) — input 'input' [1,3,H,W] f32, outputs 'mul','add','enhanced'. On a real near-black darkened street photo (luminance mean 0.054, std 0.044, range 0–0.16) the 'enhanced' output measured mean 0.375, std 0.195, range 0–0.76 — a natural, well-lit scene (screenshot reviewed: a black alley → a fully-lit daytime street). transformers.js has no `iat` class and no low-light-enhancement task, so the demo runs the ONNX graph directly via raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module Worker. Headless index build-verify (CDP driver, fresh profile → Download → ready → Enhance): real output, inference 938 ms + composite 6 ms on WASM, brightness 0.094 → 0.315 AND contrast 0.044 → 0.108 (both rose), 0 console errors, 0 network failures.
rubric.notes: IAT (BMVC 2022, ~90K params, Apache-2.0) is a learned illumination-adaptive correction, not a brightness slider. MEASURED that the repo's iat_lol_v2 export is degenerate (near-constant grey, std ~0.009) — we deliberately ship the faithful iat_lol_v1 checkpoint instead; this is disclosed in worker.js.

rubric.evidence: Output shape + semantic sanity confirmed: 'enhanced' dims [1,3,H,W] in [0,1] read back at runtime (H,W dynamic, rounded to a multiple of 8); clamped to RGBA. Semantic check: brightness (mean Rec.709 luma) rises and dynamic range is restored on a dark input (visual wipe + numbers), and the 'See inside' illumination-gain map (the 'mul' tensor) is spatially uneven — bright where the scene was darkest — which is exactly what a learned illumination correction should do and a flat gain could not.
rubric.notes: image-to-image task template (dense per-pixel output grounded in the input) fits: a lit RGB image from the dark RGB input, plus the gain/offset decomposition.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance names the exact model (Pezhgorski/IAT-ONNX, linked) + the iat_lol_v1 variant + base repo, task (image-to-image / low-light enhancement), runtime (onnxruntime-web 1.21), input [1,3,H,W] / three-output shape, dtype (fp32 with external-data sidecar, plain WASM EP, no WebGPU), size (~0.4 MB) and licence (Apache-2.0), and honestly notes transformers.js has no IAT class. inference-off-main-thread = pass (new Worker + worker.js). No invented API.
rubric.notes: The external-data (.onnx.data) loading path is stated in the How-the-API snippet and is what makes ORT-Web session creation succeed here.

rubric.evidence: Every page loads through createModelLoader. The worker fetches BOTH the ONNX and the .onnx.data THROUGH Cache Storage (iat-lowlight-onnx-cache) with content-length Responses so lib/model-cache.js scans '/Pezhgorski/IAT-ONNX/' and the shared loader auto-inits on a returning visit; the per-model clear-cache control works. First-visit driver run showed the honest Download state, then ready.
rubric.notes: The 4 ladder pages share the same modelId → shared cache → auto-init; the multi-model page loads a SECOND model (DBNet text detector) through its own loader.

rubric.evidence: loads-clean = pass (0 console errors on load). Download progress streams from the worker (combined across both files) into the loader progress bar; failures surface in the .status.err line and the loader offers Retry. Status uses role=status aria-live=polite. runs-without-webgpu-fallback: WASM-only (requiresWebGPU:false) — runs everywhere, verified on the no-GPU headless runner. A pass is sub-second (~0.9 s at 512-max) thanks to the ~90K-param model.
rubric.notes: The darkness controls only rebuild the cheap preview; inference runs only on Enhance.

rubric.evidence: Every control exercised and labelled + keyboard-operable: the rights-safe example gallery, the drop-zone + file input, the Enhance (+ Download on the practical page) buttons, the darken-mode <select> and darkness <input type=range>, and the before/after reveal (native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext '% enhanced'). Darkness controls live-update the preview; uploads bypass synthetic darkening and enhance the real photo.
rubric.notes: Honest by design — the user chooses how dark and watches IAT recover it; genuinely dark uploads (and the bundled night-city sample) are supported.

rubric.evidence: The enhanced image, the illumination-gain map, and the brightness/contrast numbers all come from the on-device ORT-web run — no canned values. The output changes with the input and darkness (visible in the wipe, the gain map, and the readout), proving it tracks the real model, not a placeholder.
rubric.notes: The copy states plainly that IAT recovers buried signal but can't invent light where pure black erased it — a rescue, not a light source; the wild page shows recovery falling as darkness approaches near-black.

rubric.evidence: images-have-alt = pass, buttons-have-names = pass, live-status-region = pass. Canvases carry role=img + descriptive aria-label (dark input / enhanced output / illumination gain map); the reveal slider is a labelled native range with aria-valuetext; the darken select and darkness slider have <label>s; gallery thumbnails carry source/licence in their accessible name. Status is role=status aria-live=polite. :focus-visible styled on the reveal, drop-zone, and range.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied for the accessible comparison slider and canvas alternatives (see guidanceConsulted).

rubric.evidence: responsive-desktop = pass, responsive-mobile = pass. CDP driver measured horizontal overflow = 0 px at BOTH desktop 1280×800 and mobile 360×740 DPR3 on the index page. Layout uses flex:1 1 300px + min-inline-size:0 two-column (image | controls) that stacks on mobile; the gallery is an auto-fill minmax grid; canvases are inline-size:100% block-size:auto; the reveal uses logical properties + touch-action:pan-y. Light + dark inherit design-system variables.
rubric.notes: The multi-model panels use the same flex two-column so the before/after detection canvases stack on mobile.

rubric.evidence: Inference AND the dense output composite (clamp tensor → RGBA, illumination-gain heatmap — the systemic hotspot invariant 15 warns about) run in the module Worker; the enhanced image and gain map are built with OffscreenCanvas and returned as transferred ImageBitmaps (zero-copy). The main thread only draws bitmaps. Measured composite 6 ms in the worker, so no main-thread long task during interaction.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied: break-up-long-tasks / identify-inp-causes — the per-pixel work is off the main thread and transferred, not cloned.

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible range slider input keyboard labels aria-valuetext, canvas role img alt, live status region, select label
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Prefer native controls with <label>; a range input is keyboard-operable and should announce its value (aria-valuetext); give informative canvases role=img + a descriptive name; use a single polite live region; style :focus-visible.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — the before/after divider is a native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext ('N% enhanced'); the darken <select> and darkness range have <label>s; every canvas has role=img + a descriptive aria-label; a single role=status aria-live=polite reports progress/results; :focus-visible rings throughout.

guidanceConsulted.query: responsive control panel two column image + controls no horizontal overflow flexbox intrinsic sizing logical properties
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use flexbox with flex:1 1 <basis> + min-inline-size:0 so wide flex items shrink instead of overflowing; size media with inline-size:100% block-size:auto; use logical properties; auto-fill/minmax grids for galleries.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — image/controls columns use flex:1 1 300px with min-inline-size:0 and stack on narrow viewports; the example gallery is a repeat(auto-fill,minmax()) grid; canvases and the reveal are inline-size:100% block-size:auto. Verified 0 px horizontal overflow at 360×740 and 1280×800.

guidanceConsulted.query: keep INP low move image inference and dense per-pixel post-processing off main thread web worker transfer
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move long main-thread JavaScript (inference + dense pixel loops) into a worker; transfer buffers/ImageBitmaps instead of cloning.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — ONNX inference, the RGBA clamp, and the illumination-gain heatmap all run in a module worker; results are transferred back as ImageBitmaps. No blocking main-thread pixel loop during interaction.

openQuestions: IAT is dynamic-shaped; a follow-up could run at full input resolution (or tile) rather than capping the long side at 512 for speed.
A WebGPU ORT-web EP path could push this into real-time per-frame webcam brightening — worth a progressive-enhancement follow-up once verifiable on a GPU runner.

followUpGoals.goal: Add a live-camera 'night mode' wild demo that brightens each captured webcam frame (user-initiated, lib/capture-ux.js) to show real-time low-light correction.
Add an exposure-correction ladder demo using the iat_exposure checkpoint (over- AND under-exposure) alongside the low-light one.

summary: New demo: on-device LOW-LIGHT ENHANCEMENT via IAT (Pezhgorski/IAT-ONNX, iat_lol_v1 checkpoint, Apache-2.0, ~0.4 MB, ~90K params) run through raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module worker, with the dense output composite (clamp + illumination-gain heatmap) off the main thread and transferred as ImageBitmaps. Runnability was proven first in headless Chrome (external-data session creation; a near-black dark street → a natural lit scene, brightness up + contrast preserved). MEASURED that the repo's iat_lol_v2 export is degenerate (flat grey) and deliberately shipped the faithful v1 instead (disclosed in worker.js). The index + 4 ladder pages (basics darken-then-brighten / practical rescue+download / wild darkness breaking-point / multi-model brighten→text-detection) were build-verified with real download + inference, an accessible before/after reveal, cached auto-init, clean console, and 0 px overflow on mobile+desktop. Sample images come from the rights-safe media library; the synthetic darken is applied at a bounded working scale, and real dark photos can be uploaded.
